purpose: Distill how the V7.2.4 slice 01-19 planning layers worked before the later overlay and Wave 1-11 governance repair.

core_conclusion.summary: The slice 01-19 process planned each slice through four cumulative layers: foundation lifecycle, contract application, semantic application, and fine-detail work orders. The layers converted a broad design bundle into a one-slice-at-a-time build sequence with explicit claims, non-claims, prerequisites, validators, fixtures, receipts, checkpoints, and next-slice gates.
core_conclusion.main_mechanic: For each slice, the planner set exactly one owned behavior true and kept downstream behaviors false until their slice. This is why the sequence could discuss local LLM, dry-run orchestration, runtime, replay, and readiness without authorizing them early.
core_conclusion.later_progression: Wave 1-11 did not replace the layered idea; it simplified and hardened it. The Wave packet/plan structure kept owner, proof, boundary, and refresh decisions, while dropping routine prompt/handoff/receipt noise.

how_it_worked: Copied the accepted four-layer stack into repo-owned JSON so planning no longer depended on Desktop-only ZIP paths.

Declared validation requirements for the bundle itself: all 77 JSON files parse, expected layer file counts hold, active plans cite the repo-owned root, and repo index discovery is current or confirmed.
Named the full 19-slice sequence and per-slice lifecycle requirements.
For each slice, declared allowed execution flags, blocker conditions, authority reads, completion criteria, receipts, checkpoints, logs, validation commands, and next-slice dependency.
Marked the bundle as design/planning only, not implementation, not tested, and not production ready.

Established timing: first live provider test at slice 06, first actual data pull at slice 07, first transformation proof at slice 08, local model structured JSON at slice 15, dry-run queue at slice 16, runtime research at slice 17, replay/configured-scope smoke at slice 18, and operator readiness report at slice 19.
layer_stack.planning_value: This layer prevented capability leakage by making every downstream behavior false until its slice owned it.

how_it_worked: Applied shared contracts across all 19 slices instead of creating one bespoke contract per slice.
Mapped each slice to required configs, schemas, validators, tests, fixtures, receipts, checkpoints, logs, real-data checks, and repo-corpus checks.

Kept global claim boundaries false: no implementation, command run, provider access, data pull, runtime research, VBT sweep, replay, configured-scope smoke, production candidate, or production ready claim by the design bundle.
layer_stack.planning_value: This made the proof surface explicit before coding and made missing contracts or required proof a blocker.

how_it_worked: Mapped semantic terms to every slice: behavior true fields, behavior false fields, required terms, supporting terms, and terms forbidden as active claims.
Defined actual planned capability tracks inside the 19 slices: local LLM structured JSON inference, full autopilot/script-sweep loop, and GPU/ML/RL expansion.
Made the semantics non-authorizing: local model output could feed deterministic validators and operator decisions but could not self-approve, mutate protected surfaces, or execute shell commands.
layer_stack.planning_value: This layer said what words meant, so phrases like runtime, replay, local model, and readiness could appear in planning without becoming premature permission.

how_it_worked: Mapped 18 fine-detail files onto all 19 slices.

For each slice, carried fine-detail scope, old pipeline stage references, proposed future design targets, required authority reads, required configs/contracts/schemas/validators/tests/fixtures/logs, positive and negative fixture requirements, receipt/checkpoint/log requirements, and validation commands.
Repeated the behavior-boundary flags and prohibited execution classes so the detailed work order could not override the upper layers.
layer_stack.planning_value: This was the closest layer to a slice packet: it translated lifecycle, contract, and semantic rules into the files and proof each slice needed.

slice_ladder_from_layers.logic: Build the language and claim boundary before provider, data, feature, runtime, replay, or readiness work.

slice_ladder_from_layers.layered_role: Catalog, provider authorization, first data, and transformation.
slice_ladder_from_layers.logic: Move from design metadata into bounded provider proof, first actual data pull, and first transformation proof.

slice_ladder_from_layers.layered_role: Feature, mask, label, and PIT lineage boundaries.
slice_ladder_from_layers.logic: Materialize decision-time features and masks, quarantine hindsight labels, and prove PIT/holdout/source-lineage rules before runtime.

slice_ladder_from_layers.layered_role: VBT Pro sweep planning and budget control.
slice_ladder_from_layers.logic: Compile native sweep axes and then run a bounded random subset under resource and receipt controls without unbounded sweep or production claims.

slice_ladder_from_layers.layered_role: Operator config loop and dry-run orchestration.
slice_ladder_from_layers.logic: Introduce model-generated structured JSON and script-sweep preview through validation, receipts, allowlists, and operator decisions without direct mutation or execution.

slice_ladder_from_layers.layered_role: Runtime research, replay/portfolio smoke, and readiness evidence.
slice_ladder_from_layers.logic: Run bounded research and replay proof only after data, feature, mask, label, PIT, sweep, queue, and operator prerequisites, then report evidence and blockers without auto-promotion.

portable_planning_pattern: Start with a fixed slice sequence and no extra capability slices unless a new authority plan says so.
For each slice, define the one owned behavior and explicitly list downstream behaviors that remain false.
Apply shared contracts across slices instead of inventing a new contract shape for every slice.
Use a semantic layer to define claim vocabulary before implementation, especially for risky terms like runtime, replay, readiness, model output, and production.
Turn the fine-detail layer into a packet-like owner bundle: authority reads, owner files, validators, focused tests, fixtures, receipts/checkpoints only when justified, and next-slice gate.
Before coding, revalidate current repo authority because the four-layer bundle itself is planning evidence, not implementation proof.

what_wave_1_11_inherited: Planning evidence kept in repo-owned JSON.
what_wave_1_11_simplified: Layer detail collapsed into smaller wave packets and active-plan notes.
Routine receipts/checkpoints became discouraged unless tied to a writer, generator, materializer, external action, irreversible decision, or closeout gate.
Generated refreshes became conditional instead of a default layer validation habit.
Historical design layers became evidence, while current active plans/packets controlled next action.
relationship_to_wave_1_11.bottom_line: The 1-19 layers were a rich design-to-slice compiler. Wave 1-11 was the governance compression pass that kept the useful fields and removed the noisy authority ambiguity.
